*   Abstract
--------

[![Image 35](https://pubs.acs.org/cms/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509/asset/images/medium/au5c01509_0008.gif)](https://doi.org/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509#_i1)
Figure 1
--------

[![Image 36](https://pubs.acs.org/cms/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509/asset/images/medium/au5c01509_0001.gif)](https://doi.org/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509#fig1)

Figure 1. Working principle of AmyCAT. The AmyCAT photocatalyst is designed to selectively bind amyloid proteins and undergo Dexter energy transfer (DET) to catalyze protein proximity labeling of amyloid plaques in Alzheimer’s disease (AD) brain tissues. Upon intercalating into rigid amyloid and inhibiting bond rotations, DET photocatalysis is activated to convert the diazo substrate into a reactive carbene intermediate. The hyperactive and short-lived carbene is confined to label amyloid and interacting proteins in proximity to the photocatalyst molecule but rapidly quenched for the far-end noninteracting proteome. The DET-based AmyCAT photocatalyst allows for _in situ_ proteomics profiling and identification of the composition of amyloid deposits with spatial resolution.

Figure 2
--------

[![Image 37](https://pubs.acs.org/cms/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509/asset/images/medium/au5c01509_0002.gif)](https://doi.org/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509#fig2)

Figure 2. Rational design of AmyCAT to catalyze amyloid proximity labeling. (a) Scheme to illustrate the selection criteria of the photocatalyst/substrate pair. (b) Structures of photocatalysts designed from the FP chromophore and potential substrates to generate carbene and nitrene for protein labeling. (c) FP chromophores intrinsically bound and partitioned into insoluble protein aggregates. [Probe]: 20 μM, [amorphous aggregated DHFR]: 100 μM, and [amyloid aggregated TTR]: 100 μM. (d) Dissociation constants (_K_ d) of FP chromophores and amyloid Aβ simulated by molecular docking. The molecular structures were optimized by Gaussian 09, and the docking results were simulated by AutoDockTools 1.5.7. (e) Simulated binding mode between A2B1 and Aβ amyloid (PDB code [8OVM](https://doi.org/10.2210/pdb8OVM/pdb)). (f) Schematic diagram to illustrate the DET process from both the electron pathway and energy pathway. As shown in the electron pathway, the Dexter energy transfer (DET) mechanism operates through concerted two-electron exchange, represented by the state transition, from [photocatalyst (triplet excited state)|substrate (ground state)] to [photocatalyst (ground state)|substrate (triplet excited state)]. Therefore, as shown in the energy pathway, thermodynamically, this process is favorable when the _E_ T1 of the substrate is lower than that of the photocatalyst. After calculating the _E_ T1 of A2B1 and four candidate substrates, only S3 only exhibited a slightly lower _E_ T1 than that of A2B1. _E_ T1 = triplet excited energy. (g) Mismatched spectra between S3 absorption (blue lines) and A2B1 emission (red line). [A2B1]: 50 μM; solvent: DMSO. (h) Fluorescence intensity of A2B1 gradually decreased upon increasing the S3 concentration. [A2B1]: 50 μM; solvent: DMSO.

Figure 3
--------

[![Image 38](https://pubs.acs.org/cms/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509/asset/images/medium/au5c01509_0003.gif)](https://doi.org/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509#fig3)

Figure 3. When A2B1 and S3 diffuse freely in solution, S3 cannot be catalyzed to carbene by A2B1. (a) Schematic illustration of direct photolysis or DET photocatalysis to activate diazo into carbene. (b) 1 H NMR spectra of S3 before and after photoactivation in CD 3 OD at given conditions. The chemical shift of the ester methyl group (H a and H b) was monitored for product conversion. Illumination time: 5 h; illumination intensity: 6 mW cm–2; S3: 10 mg, 72 mM, 1 equiv; A2B1: 14 mM, 0.2 equiv; CD 3 OD: 600 μL. Detailed analysis of the 1 H spectra is shown in [Figure S5](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/suppl/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509/suppl_file/au5c01509_si_001.pdf).

Figure 4
--------

[![Image 39](https://pubs.acs.org/cms/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509/asset/images/medium/au5c01509_0004.gif)](https://doi.org/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509#fig4)

Figure 4. Photocatalysis of A2B1/S3 is promoted by both spatial confinement and the restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR). (a) Photocatalysis via DET is triggered by covalently tethering in the A2B1–S3 photocatalyst–substrate conjugate. (b) 1 H NMR spectra of A2B1–S3 with/without 5 h of green LED illumination in methanol. The chemical shift of the ester methyl group (H a and H b) was monitored to track the product formation. Illumination time: 5 h; illumination intensity: 6 mW cm–2. Detailed analysis of the 1 H spectra is shown in [Figure S7](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/suppl/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509/suppl_file/au5c01509_si_001.pdf). (c) Restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) in a viscous solvent improved the efficiency of the DET process in A2B1–S3. (d) A2B1–S3 conversion was monitored by HPLC. Samples were dissolved in fluid MeOH/DMSO (3:7) or viscous glycerol/DMSO (3:7). Illumination intensity: 6 mW cm–2. [A2B1–S3]: 1 mg mL–1. Due to the poor solubility of A2B1–S3, the maximum volume fraction of glycerol can reach only reach 30%. Detailed analysis of the HPLC results is shown in [Figure S8](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/suppl/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509/suppl_file/au5c01509_si_001.pdf). (e) A2B1–S3 conversion rate calculated from HPLC. (f) Kinetic fittings of the conversion rate of A2B1–S3 in nonviscous MeOH/DMSO (3:7) or viscous glycerol/DMSO (3:7).

Figure 5
--------

[![Image 40](https://pubs.acs.org/cms/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509/asset/images/medium/au5c01509_0005.gif)](https://doi.org/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509#fig5)

Figure 5. AmyCAT selectively photocatalyzed proximity labeling of aggregated proteins. (a) When A2B1 and S3 are adsorbed into porous aggregated proteins, the spatial distance between them becomes closer. The intramolecular rotation of A2B1 is also restricted by the rigid microenvironment in aggregates, triggering DET photocatalysis. (b) Scheme to quantify the photocatalytic labeling efficiency. [AmyCAT]: 10 μM; [substrate]: 50 μM; [aggregated DHFR]: 50 μM; illumination intensity: 6 mW cm–2; illumination time: 20 min. The labeling efficiency was quantified by ImageJ. (c) Quantified labeling efficiency of the 12 × 4 photocatalyst/substrate pairs toward aggregated DHFR. (d) Quantified labeling efficiency of A2B1/S3 toward aggregated protein upon illumination by light sources spanning different wavelengths. Error bars: standard error (_n_ = 3). (e) AmyCAT exhibited selectivity for catalyzing labeling of aggregated proteins over folded ones upon green LED illumination. F = folded. A = aggregated. [DHFR]: 50 μM. [Halo]: 50 μM. [Tau]: 100 μM. [α-Syn]: 100 μM. Error bars: standard error (_n_ = 3). (f) PPL mediated by DET provided a higher S/N ratio for aggregated DHFR labeling compared to that mediated by reactive oxygen species (ROS) using folded halo as a counterpart. [AmyCAT]: 10 μM; [aggregated DHFR]: 50 μM; [folded halo]: 50 μM; [S3]: 50 μM; [PA]: 10 mM. Error bars: standard error (_n_ = 3).

Figure 6
--------

[![Image 41](https://pubs.acs.org/cms/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509/asset/images/medium/au5c01509_0006.gif)](https://doi.org/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509#fig6)

Figure 6. AmyCAT photocatalyzed labeling of amyloid deposits in Alzheimer’s disease tissues. (a) Schematic illustration of the photocatalytic labeling procedures using AD brain tissues. [AmyCAT]: 10 μM; [substrate]: 50 μM; illumination intensity: 6 mW cm–2; illumination time: 20 min. (b) Colocalization of fluorescence images of AD tissues stained by ThS (blue fluorescence) and A2B1 (red fluorescence). (c) Proximity labeling region (blue fluorescence upon clicking to coumarin-N 3) colocalized well with A2B1 (red fluorescence). (d) Schematic of the photocatalytic mechanism of HRP, AggID, and AmyCAT techniques. (e) HRP, AggID, and AmyCAT labeled Aβ _in vitro_ effectively. (f) HRP, AggID, and AmyCAT labeled amyloid plaques on AD brain tissue sections effectively with different colocalization. (g) Comparison of the labeling radius and precision by calculating the Pearson correlation coefficient for colocalizing ThS amyloid signal and TMR labeling signal. Error bars: standard error (_n_ = 5). **_P_< 0.01; *_P_< 0.05.

Figure 7
--------

[![Image 42](https://pubs.acs.org/cms/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509/asset/images/medium/au5c01509_0007.gif)](https://doi.org/10.1021/jacsau.5c01509#fig7)

Figure 7. AmyCAT photocatalyzed selective labeling of amyloid deposits in Alzheimer’s disease tissues for proteomic profiling application. (a) Schematic illustration of the photocatalytic labeling and proteomic profiling procedures using AD brain tissues. [AmyCAT]: 10 μM; [substrate]: 50 μM; illumination intensity: 6 mW cm–2; illumination time: 20 min. (b) Volcano plot of the significantly differentially upregulated/enriched proteins in the AD mouse brain. (c) Comparison of the enrichment fold among direct homogenization, laser capture microdissection, and this work. All data sets used APP/PS1 mice as the AD model. (d) Target validation by immunofluorescence colocalization imaging of identified AD-associated biomarkers (APP, APOE, GFAP, CLU, TAPBP, and FGA) stained by antibodies and amyloid deposits stained by ThS. (e) Gene ontology (GO) analysis of the biological process of the enriched proteins. (f) Key proteins identified by AmyCAT can be attributed to established pathways (chaperone, lipid metabolism, inflammation, immunity, etc.) related to AD etiology reported in the literature.

# 任务
从以下论文内容中提取信息，以 JSON 格式返回。

## 严格遵守规则：绝对不要提取 References 和 Cite This Article 部分的任何信息：

- References 和 Cite This Article 中的作者姓名、单位、联系方式
- References 和 Cite This Article 中的文章标题、DOI
- References 和 Cite This Article 中的任何其他信息
- 只提取正文内容（References 和 Cite This Article 之前的部分）


## 发表日期
提取 Issue published 或 Published Time 对应的日期。如果论文同时存在 Received、Accepted、Published 等多个日期，只取发表日期，其他日期忽略。


## DOI
提取论文的 DOI，统一使用完整 URL 格式，例如 https://doi.org/10.1234/xxx。如果原文只有编号形式如 10.1234/xxx，则补全为 https://doi.org/10.1234/xxx。


## 作者与符号规则

作者列表通常位于标题下方，例如：
Weiping Yang 1,#, Wei Xiao 2,#, Wenhao Xu 3,#, Lijun Ren 1, Xian Li 1, Junhua Yu 1, Ronghua Wang 4*

符号含义如下：

数字上标表示作者所属机构编号，对应文末的机构列表。一个作者如果同时属于多个机构，就会有多个数字，比如 3,4。# 和 † 都表示共同第一作者，意味着这几位作者对论文贡献相同，并列第一，两者含义相同，不同期刊用法不同。* 表示通讯作者，负责与期刊沟通以及接收读者问询，通常是导师或负责人。以上符号可以同时出现在一个作者名字后面。

示例：

Wenhao Xu 3,# 表示该作者来自第3机构，且是共同第一作者。

Tao Li 3,4,* 表示该作者同时来自第3和第4机构，且是通讯作者。

Wei Zhao 1,3,* 表示该作者同时来自第1和第3机构，且也是通讯作者，与 Tao Li 并列通讯作者。

Wang Fang 1,#,* 表示该作者来自第1机构，同时是共同第一作者，也是通讯作者，一个人身兼两个角色。

### 脚注说明
所有符号的具体含义以论文脚注说明为准。脚注通常没有标题，直接列出符号对应的说明文字，位置一般紧跟在作者姓名和机构列表的下方，内容通常如下：
- # These authors contributed equally to this work
- † These authors have contributed equally to this work
- * Corresponding author: email@xxx.com

通讯作者的邮箱也可能出现在文末的 Correspondence、Contact 或 *Correspondence 区域，而不是作者列表下方的脚注，需要一并查找。如果邮箱字段存在但内容为空，同样填 null。

如果脚注缺失或格式不标准，按照以下降级处理：* 默认视为通讯作者，# 和 † 默认视为共同第一作者，数字上标默认对应机构编号。如果论文完全没有任何符号标注，所有作者共享同一机构地址，则联系人取作者列表中排名第一的作者。


## 地址
机构和地理地址合并在同一字段中。地址编号与作者名称后的数字上标关联对应。例如 Lijun Ren 1，他的地址信息对应：1 Department of Breast and Thyroid Surgery, Qingdao Chengyang People's Hospital, Qingdao, 266109, China。

如果机构名称中含有斜杠，如 Rudong People's Hospital/Affiliated Rudong Hospital of Xinglin College，斜杠表示同一机构的两种称呼或并列名称，翻译时保留斜杠结构，不拆分为两个地址。


## 联系人识别规则

优先提取通讯作者，通讯作者通常用 * 标注。如果有多位通讯作者，提取排名第一的那位，例如 Jike Song 1,4* 和 Hongsheng Bi 1,2,3,4* 并列通讯作者，则提取排名靠前的 Jike Song。如果论文中没有通讯作者，则提取第一作者，第一作者是作者列表中排名第一的人。如果第一作者有多人（即共同第一作者，用 # 或 † 标注），则提取其中排名第一的那位。如果论文既没有通讯作者标注也没有第一作者信息，或者论文没有任何符号标注，则默认提取作者列表中的第一个人。


## 中国作者识别规则

**A. 姓名判断（中文拼音规则）**：支持复姓。

**B. 地址判断（中国机构）**：地址字段包含关键词 china（不区分大小写）。

**C. 组合判断规则**：同时满足姓名符合中文拼音规则且地址包含中国关键词，视为中国作者。

**D. 排除规则**：地址同时包含 "USA"、"UK"、"Japan" 等其他国家关键词以及 "China"，仍视为中国（如 "China-USA 联合研究所"）。


## 字段对应关系
corresponding_author 下的 address、email、phone 必须对应联系人这个人，不是其他作者的信息。


## 翻译要求

把 address 字段翻译并放到 address_cn 字段。每个完整地址的格式从科室→机构→城市→邮编→国家。所有地址均需翻译，包括非中国机构（如美国、日本等）也需翻译成中文。

示例：
- 四川大学华西医院心内科，成都 610041，中国
- 四川省人民医院心内科，成都 610072，中国

Title: Global research trends and foci of ablation therapies for liver tumours: a scientometric study

Original Article

Highlight box
-------------

**Key findings**

• This scientometric study mapped the global research landscape of ablation therapies for liver tumours from 1991 to 2022, showing a sustained and rapid growth in publication output.

• Research activity is concentrated in a limited number of countries and institutions, with the USA and China as leading contributors and Sun Yat-sen University and Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center as major hubs.

• Thematic evolution reveals a clear shift from technique-driven research (e.g., radiofrequency ablation) toward outcome-oriented and integrative topics, including overall survival, irreversible electroporation, cryoablation and immunotherapy.

**What is known and what is new?**

• Ablation therapies are an important and rapidly developing component of liver tumour management, with extensive clinical and technological research activity worldwide.

• This manuscript adds a systematic, data-driven mapping of collaboration networks, influential countries, institutions, and authors, and clearly documents the temporal evolution of research themes, identifying immunotherapy-related and combination-based strategies as emerging research frontiers.

**What is the implication, and what should change now?**

• The findings offer researchers a structured framework to understand the current landscape of liver tumour ablation research and to identify opportunities for collaboration and innovation.

• Future research should emphasize broader international cooperation and place greater focus on outcome-driven and combination-based approaches that integrate ablation with systemic and immunological therapies.

Liver tumours, encompassing both primary liver cancer and secondary hepatic malignancies, represent a substantial global health challenge and a leading cause of cancer-related fatalities ([1](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B1),[2](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B2)). Traditional treatment modalities, such as surgical resection and liver transplantation, have limitations, leaving a significant proportion of patients (more than 75%) with limited options due to factors such as locally advanced diseases, tumour size, location, or comorbidities such as underlying liver diseases ([3](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B3),[4](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B4)). In recent years, the field of minimally invasive ablation therapies, including radiofrequency ablation (RFA), microwave ablation (MWA), and irreversible electroporation (IRE), has emerged as a promising alternative for the management of liver tumours in carefully selected patient cohorts ([5](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B5)-[7](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B7)).

Ablation therapy, a well-established and minimally invasive approach, utilizes image-guided techniques for the treatment of both primary and secondary liver cancers ([8](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B8),[9](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B9)). Its advantages over conventional surgical interventions, such as reduced invasiveness, shorter hospital stays, and faster recovery times ([10](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B10),[11](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B11)), have positioned it as a valuable treatment option, and its versatility allows for repeated procedures and combination with other treatment modalities, enhancing its utility in multidisciplinary liver tumour management ([12](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B12),[13](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B13)). As the field of ablation therapy continues to evolve, gaining insights into the global research landscape, identifying key trends, and understanding the foci of investigation are of paramount importance.

Scientometric studies offer a systematic framework for analyzing scientific literature, providing valuable insights into publication patterns, identifying leading contributors, and uncovering emerging research themes ([14](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B14)). By employing scientometric analysis, this study aims to comprehensively assess the research trends and focal points of ablation therapy for liver tumours. This approach allows us to quantitatively visualize the current state of research, including contributions from countries, institutions, and authors, journal preferences, and emerging research directions. In particular, the co-word analysis of keywords within this field not only provides detailed information about subdisciplines but also reveals intrinsic associations among them, offering a holistic view of the evolving research landscape ([15](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B15)). The detection of co-existing keywords, or co-words, over time signifies emerging areas of interest that hold current and future significance ([16](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B16)).

While bibliometric analyses have been employed successfully to evaluate research trends in various medical disciplines and specialties, including gastric diseases, infectious diseases, and urinary diseases ([17](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B17)-[19](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B19)), an exploration of the literature trends within the context of ablation therapies for liver tumours has been notably absent. Our study aims to bridge this gap by employing bibliometric and scientometric analysis techniques, such as co-word matrix analysis and biclustering, to shed light on domestic research in the field of ablation therapy for liver cancer and its subdisciplines, track their recent evolution, and uncover future development trends. Through this comprehensive analysis, we aim to provide a foundational understanding of the global research landscape in liver tumour ablation therapies, stimulate international collaboration among researchers, and ultimately advance treatment strategies for liver tumours, thereby enhancing patient care. We present this article in accordance with the BIBLIO reporting checklist (available at [https://jgo.amegroups.com/article/view/10.21037/jgo-2025-750/rc](https://jgo.amegroups.com/article/view/10.21037/jgo-2025-750/rc)).

### Data source, retrieval, extraction, and validation

The Web of Science (WOS) Core Collection was searched without imposing publication date restrictions, and the primary search was executed on September 30, 2022. An advanced search strategy was employed using the following keyword combinations: (“Ablation”) AND (“Cancer” or “Tumour” or “Neoplasm”) AND (“liver” or “hepatic”), with document type specified as “Article”. Inclusion criteria were: (I) publications explicitly related to ablation therapies for liver tumors; (II) studies addressing clinical applications, outcomes, technological advancements, or combination strategies involving ablation; and (III) articles published in peer-reviewed journals indexed in the WOS Core Collection. Exclusion criteria included non-English publications, conference abstracts, review articles, editorial materials, and records not related to liver tumors or ablation technologies.

The initial search yielded 11,678 records. After applying document-type filters and screening titles and abstracts for relevance, 5,970 original research articles were retained for analysis. All retained records were downloaded in plain text format with complete records and cited references. To ensure data accuracy and relevance, two authors independently screened and validated the retrieved records, paying particular attention to the titles and abstracts. Any discrepancies were resolved through discussion with a third author until consensus was reached. To assess the robustness of the search strategy and document-type filtering, the search was subsequently rerun using alternative spellings (e.g., “tumor” instead of “tumour”) and an updated cutoff date of December 31, 2022. These supplementary searches yielded higher overall record counts and article totals, reflecting spelling variants, database indexing updates, and differences in cutoff dates within the WOS Core Collection. Notably, the proportion of original research articles remained consistent across searches, supporting the validity and representativeness of the analytic dataset used in this study. All bibliometric analyses were conducted using the dataset downloaded on September 30, 2022.

### Scientometric analysis tools and preprocessing

The validated dataset was imported into CiteSpace (Version 5.1.R3, Drexel University, USA) for bibliometric analysis. During data preprocessing, CiteSpace automatically identified and removed duplicate records based on bibliographic metadata, including title, author, and publication year ([20](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B20)). To visualize annual publication trends, GraphPad Prism 9.0 (USA) was employed. CiteSpace was employed to analyze publication characteristics, including countries/regions, institutions, authors, journals, and keywords ([20](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B20)), as well as to perform co-citation and citation burst analyses (using the burst-detection algorithm). VOSviewer (Version 1.6.16) and the online platform ([http://bibliometric.com](http://bibliometric.com/)) were used to construct and analyze collaboration networks and keyword co-occurrence networks.

### Co-authorship network analysis

Co-authorship data were extracted from the retained publications. Author names, institutional affiliations, and country/region information were recorded for each article. Collaboration relationships were defined based on co-authorship within the same publication. To improve network interpretability and reduce noise, minimum occurrence thresholds were applied at the country/region, institution, and author levels. For author-level analysis, a minimum threshold of 14 publications was used to identify core contributors. Network visualization and clustering were performed using VOSviewer, and collaboration strength was quantified using the total link strength (TLS) metric.

### Keyword co-occurrence, clustering, and burst analysis

Keyword co-occurrence and clustering analyses were conducted using the “clustering” function in CiteSpace. Keyword clusters were generated using the Pathfinder algorithm and the log-likelihood ratio (LLR) method. Network quality was assessed using modularity (Q) and silhouette (S) scores; Q>0.3 indicates significant cluster structure, and S>0.7 indicates high cluster homogeneity. Citation burst detection was applied to identify emerging research topics. A time slice of four years was selected to balance temporal resolution and network stability over the long study period. The g-index (k=3) was used as the node selection criterion to limit the influence of highly cited nodes and reduce network complexity. Timezone visualization was used to illustrate the temporal evolution of research themes.

### Country/region, institutional, journal, and citation analysis

Country/region- and institution-level collaboration analyses were performed using VOSviewer based on shared co-authorship. TLS values were used to quantify collaboration intensity. Journals were ranked according to publication volume, and citation and co-citation analyses were conducted to identify influential journals and articles within the field. Co-citation networks were visualized using CiteSpace and VOSviewer.

### Statistical analysis

Descriptive statistical analyses were used to summarize publication characteristics, including annual publication output, country/region and institutional productivity, author contributions, journal distributions, and citation counts. Continuous variables are presented as counts, percentages, and trends over time. Linear regression analysis was performed using GraphPad Prism 9.0 (GraphPad Software, USA) to evaluate temporal trends in annual publication output, with the coefficient of determination (R 2) used to assess goodness of fit. Bibliometric network analyses were conducted using CiteSpace (Version 5.1.R3, Drexel University, USA) and VOSviewer (Version 1.6.16). As this study is based on bibliometric and scientometric analyses of published literature, no inferential statistical comparisons between experimental groups were performed, and no hypothesis testing was required.

### Global publication trends

A total of 5,970 articles on liver tumour ablation published between 1991 and 2022 were included in the analysis. Annual publication output demonstrated a strong, sustained upward trend, with an average yearly publication volume of 186.56 articles (R 2=0.9005; [_Figure 1_](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#figure1)). Three distinct developmental phases were identified: an early exploratory phase [1991–1996], a period of stable growth [1997–2002], and a rapid expansion phase beginning in 2003, marking a period of rapid expansion in liver tumour ablation research. Since the early 2000s, research output has increased steadily, reflecting the maturation of liver tumour ablation as a research field.

**Figure 1** Annual publication trends in liver tumour ablation research from 1991 to 2022. The figure shows the yearly number of publications retrieved from the Web of Science Core Collection. Linear regression analysis demonstrates a strong upward trend in publication output (R 2=0.9005).

### Contributing countries/regions analysis

A total of 82 countries or regions contributed to the liver tumour ablation research ([_Table 1_](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#table1)). The USA ranked first in publication volume (1,741 articles; 28.9%), total citations (n=94,887), and number of collaborative links (n=55), followed by China, Germany, Japan, and Italy. The USA, China, and Germany also exhibited the highest TLS values of 894, 377, and 478, respectively, indicating extensive international collaboration. Although the UK and Italy produced fewer publications, they demonstrated comparatively high average citation rates (60.8 and 60.4 citations per document, respectively), indicating high average citation impact. Country/region collaboration mapping revealed three major international cooperation clusters, with dense collaborative ties among the USA, China, Germany, Italy, and Japan ([_Figure 2_](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#figure2)).

### Contributing institutions

A total of 105 institutions contributed to the published literature on liver tumour ablation ([_Table 2_](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#table2)). Sun Yat-sen University, Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Fudan University, Chinese People’s Liberation Army General Hospital, and Sungkyunkwan University were the most productive institutions, contributing 158, 117, 91, 89, and 79 documents, respectively. Collaboration network analysis identified four major institutional clusters, with Sun Yat-sen University and Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center serving as central hubs within their respective clusters ([_Figure 3_](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#figure3)). These findings indicate that research output is concentrated within a limited number of highly collaborative institutions.

### Contributing authors

Between 1991 and 2022, 29,260 authors contributed to liver tumour ablation research. In accordance with Price’s Law, a core group of 172 (Price’s Law square root =171.68) authors accounted for 50.7% (n=3,026) of all publications. The most prolific authors were Ping Liang, Jie Yu, Masatoshi Kudo, S. Nahum Goldberg, and Timothy M. Pawlik, who contributed 76, 53, 49, 45, and 45 articles, respectively ([_Table 3_](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#table3)). Ping Liang and Jie Yu exhibited the highest TLS values of 226 and 185, respectively, indicating their central roles within collaborative author networks ([_Figure 4_](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#figure4)). Masatoshi Kudo had the highest number of total citations (n=3,071), indicating high citation impact within the field.

### Publishing journals and disciplinary distribution

Research on liver tumour ablation was disseminated across a wide range of journals ([_Table 4_](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#table4)). The _International Journal of Hyperthermia_, _Annals of Surgical Oncology_, _Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology_, and _Cardiovascular and Interventional Radiology_ were the most frequent publication venues. However, the top 10 journals collectively accounted for less than 20% of total publications. Dual-map overlay analysis demonstrated that liver tumour ablation research is primarily published within molecular, clinical, and health science journals (_Z_ value =8.765), with citation linkages concentrated in pharmacology, biology, and health sciences ([_Figure 5_](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#figure5)).

### Keyword analysis and thematic evolution

Keyword co-occurrence analysis identified “hepatocellular carcinoma” and “radiofrequency ablation” as the most frequent and central keywords within the research network, with counts of 1,086 and 810, respectively ([_Figure 6_](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#figure6); [_Table 5_](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#table5)). Keyword clustering analysis revealed 11 major thematic clusters, primarily focused on ablation techniques, treatment efficacy, survival outcomes, and adjunctive therapies ([_Figure 7_](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#figure7), [Table S1](https://cdn.amegroups.cn/static/public/JGO-2025-750-Supplementary.pdf?v=1773366772587)).

Temporal burst and Timezone analyses demonstrated a clear evolution in research focus over time. Early research was dominated by technique- and procedure-oriented keywords, including “radiofrequency ablation” and “cryoablation-related” terms. This was followed by a phase emphasizing clinical evaluation and procedural integration, such as imaging guidance, prognostic factors, and combined locoregional approaches. In recent years, outcome-oriented and integrative themes have emerged, including overall survival, IRE, and immunotherapy-related terms ([_Figures 8,9_](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#figure8)[](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#figure9)). Together, these patterns indicate a progressive shift from technique-driven to outcome-oriented and combination-focused research themes within the field.

Discussion Other Section
------------------------

*   [Introduction](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#introduction)
*   [Methods](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#methods)
*   [Results](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#results)
*   [Discussion](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#discussion)
*   [Conclusions](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#conclusions)
*   [Acknowledgments](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#acknowledgments)
*   [Footnote](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#Footnote)
*   [References](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#references)

Ablation therapies are widely studied locoregional treatment approaches in liver tumours, prompting sustained research interest across clinical and translational domains ([5](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B5)-[7](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B7)). This Scientometric analysis provides an overview of the global research landscape in liver tumor ablation over the past three decades. The results reveal sustained growth in publication output, extensive international collaboration, and a clear evolution of research themes, reflecting the field’s progressive maturation from technical exploration toward more outcome-oriented and integrative research directions ([21](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B21)).

Temporal keyword and citation burst analyses indicate three broad phases in the development of liver tumour ablation research. Early studies were dominated by technique- and procedure-oriented keywords, including “radiofrequency ablation” and “cryoablation-related” terms, reflecting an initial focus on technological development. Subsequent research emphasized clinical evaluation, with increased attention to imaging guidance, prognostic factors, and treatment outcomes. In recent years, emerging keywords such as “overall survival”, “irreversible electroporation”, and “immunotherapy” have experienced intense citation bursts, indicating a shift in scholarly attention toward longer-term outcomes and combination-focused research. Importantly, these findings reflect changes in research focus rather than evidence of clinical efficacy.

Collaboration network analyses further demonstrate that liver tumour ablation research is characterized by a high degree of internationalization, with a relatively small number of countries/regions and institutions contributing disproportionately to the literature. Core contributors, including institutions such as Sun Yat-sen University and Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, occupy central positions within collaborative networks. This concentration highlights potential geographic and institutional imbalances and suggests that future research efforts may benefit from broader cross-regional and cross-institutional participation.

From a bibliometric perspective, the recent emergence of immunotherapy-related keywords represents one of the most prominent research frontiers in liver tumour ablation. Citation burst and Timezone analyses indicate that immunotherapy-related terms appear only in the most recent phase of the literature, distinguishing them from earlier technique- or procedure-focused topics. The increasing prominence of immunotherapy-related keywords likely reflects growing scholarly attention to combination-oriented research directions discussed in the broader basic and clinical literature ([22](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B22)-[24](https://doi.org/10.21037/jgo-2025-750#B24)). Nevertheless, bibliometric analysis does not assess underlying therapeutic mechanisms or clinical efficacy; rather, it delineates shifts in research focus and identifies areas of intensified academic exploration.

Several limitations should be acknowledged. This study relied on publications in the WOS Core Collection and included only English-language articles, potentially excluding relevant studies from other databases or languages. In addition, bibliometric indicators reflect patterns of research activity and citation behavior rather than direct clinical evidence. Despite these limitations, the present analysis provides a structured, data-driven overview of the evolution, collaboration patterns, and emerging themes in liver tumour ablation research. These insights may inform future bibliometric investigations and help contextualize ongoing developments within the broader oncology research landscape. Future bibliometric studies may further examine whether emerging themes—such as technological integration and earlier disease detection—translate into sustained shifts in research focus within liver tumour ablation.

In conclusion, this bibliometric analysis provides a structured overview of the global research landscape in liver tumour ablation over the past three decades. The findings demonstrate sustained growth in publication output, increasing international collaboration, and a clear evolution in research themes within the field. Analyses of collaboration networks and keyword patterns highlight the concentration of research activity among leading countries/regions and institutions, as well as the diversification and maturation of research topics over time. By identifying research gaps and emerging areas of scholarly attention, this study offers a data-driven foundation for further bibliometric and research trend analyses. Continued collaborative efforts and focused investigation may further advance understanding of research priorities and thematic developments in liver tumour ablation.

## 作者与符号规则

作者列表通常位于标题下方，例如：
Weiping Yang 1,#, Wei Xiao 2,#, Wenhao Xu 3,#, Lijun Ren 1, Xian Li 1, Junhua Yu 1, Ronghua Wang 4*

符号含义如下：

数字上标表示作者所属机构编号，对应文末的机构列表。一个作者如果同时属于多个机构，就会有多个数字，比如 3,4。# 和 † 都表示共同第一作者，意味着这几位作者对论文贡献相同，并列第一，两者含义相同，不同期刊用法不同。* 表示通讯作者，负责与期刊沟通以及接收读者问询，通常是导师或负责人。以上符号可以同时出现在一个作者名字后面。

示例：

Wenhao Xu 3,# 表示该作者来自第3机构，且是共同第一作者。

Tao Li 3,4,* 表示该作者同时来自第3和第4机构，且是通讯作者。

Wei Zhao 1,3,* 表示该作者同时来自第1和第3机构，且也是通讯作者，与 Tao Li 并列通讯作者。

Wang Fang 1,#,* 表示该作者来自第1机构，同时是共同第一作者，也是通讯作者，一个人身兼两个角色。

### 脚注说明
所有符号的具体含义以论文脚注说明为准。脚注通常没有标题，直接列出符号对应的说明文字，位置一般紧跟在作者姓名和机构列表的下方，内容通常如下：
- # These authors contributed equally to this work
- † These authors have contributed equally to this work
- * Corresponding author: email@xxx.com

通讯作者的邮箱也可能出现在文末的 Correspondence、Contact 或 *Correspondence 区域，而不是作者列表下方的脚注，需要一并查找。如果邮箱字段存在但内容为空，同样填 null。

如果脚注缺失或格式不标准，按照以下降级处理：* 默认视为通讯作者，# 和 † 默认视为共同第一作者，数字上标默认对应机构编号。如果论文完全没有任何符号标注，所有作者共享同一机构地址，则联系人取作者列表中排名第一的作者。

Abstract
--------

Radiotherapy resistance remains a major barrier to effective treatment of triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), highlighting the need to identify mechanisms driving resistance. Here, we identified SDC1 as a pivotal mediator of cancer-associated fibroblast (CAF)-induced radioresistance in breast cancer. SDC1 bound the TIM barrel domain of the glycolytic enzyme ENO1, preventing FBXW7-mediated degradation and driving aerobic glycolysis and lactate accumulation. The resulting lactate-rich microenvironment not only promoted tumor stemness but also significantly impaired the cytotoxic functions of both NK cells and CD8⁺ T cells. Pharmacologic inhibition of ENO1 or lactate export restored radiosensitivity. Targeting SDC1⁺ CAFs with the antibody-drug conjugate indatuximab ravtansine (BT062) synergized with radiotherapy in vivo, markedly reducing tumor burden, depleting stem-like tumor cells, and remodeling the immune microenvironment. These findings define a CAF metabolic program that fuels tumor stemness and rewires the immune microenvironment to confer radioresistance, supporting the therapeutic targeting of SDC1⁺ CAFs in TNBC.

# 任务
从以下论文内容中提取信息，以 JSON 格式返回。

## 严格遵守规则：绝对不要提取 References 和 Cite This Article 部分的任何信息：

- References 和 Cite This Article 中的作者姓名、单位、联系方式
- References 和 Cite This Article 中的文章标题、DOI
- References 和 Cite This Article 中的任何其他信息
- 只提取正文内容（References 和 Cite This Article 之前的部分）


## 发表日期
提取 Issue published 或 Published Time 对应的日期。如果论文同时存在 Received、Accepted、Published 等多个日期，只取发表日期，其他日期忽略。


## DOI
提取论文的 DOI，统一使用完整 URL 格式，例如 https://doi.org/10.1234/xxx。如果原文只有编号形式如 10.1234/xxx，则补全为 https://doi.org/10.1234/xxx。


## 作者与符号规则

作者列表通常位于标题下方，例如：
Weiping Yang 1,#, Wei Xiao 2,#, Wenhao Xu 3,#, Lijun Ren 1, Xian Li 1, Junhua Yu 1, Ronghua Wang 4*

符号含义如下：

数字上标表示作者所属机构编号，对应文末的机构列表。一个作者如果同时属于多个机构，就会有多个数字，比如 3,4。# 和 † 都表示共同第一作者，意味着这几位作者对论文贡献相同，并列第一，两者含义相同，不同期刊用法不同。* 表示通讯作者，负责与期刊沟通以及接收读者问询，通常是导师或负责人。以上符号可以同时出现在一个作者名字后面。

示例：

Wenhao Xu 3,# 表示该作者来自第3机构，且是共同第一作者。

Tao Li 3,4,* 表示该作者同时来自第3和第4机构，且是通讯作者。

Wei Zhao 1,3,* 表示该作者同时来自第1和第3机构，且也是通讯作者，与 Tao Li 并列通讯作者。

Wang Fang 1,#,* 表示该作者来自第1机构，同时是共同第一作者，也是通讯作者，一个人身兼两个角色。

### 脚注说明
所有符号的具体含义以论文脚注说明为准。脚注通常没有标题，直接列出符号对应的说明文字，位置一般紧跟在作者姓名和机构列表的下方，内容通常如下：
- # These authors contributed equally to this work
- † These authors have contributed equally to this work
- * Corresponding author: email@xxx.com

通讯作者的邮箱也可能出现在文末的 Correspondence、Contact 或 *Correspondence 区域，而不是作者列表下方的脚注，需要一并查找。如果邮箱字段存在但内容为空，同样填 null。

如果脚注缺失或格式不标准，按照以下降级处理：* 默认视为通讯作者，# 和 † 默认视为共同第一作者，数字上标默认对应机构编号。如果论文完全没有任何符号标注，所有作者共享同一机构地址，则联系人取作者列表中排名第一的作者。


## 地址
机构和地理地址合并在同一字段中。地址编号与作者名称后的数字上标关联对应。例如 Lijun Ren 1，他的地址信息对应：1 Department of Breast and Thyroid Surgery, Qingdao Chengyang People's Hospital, Qingdao, 266109, China。

如果机构名称中含有斜杠，如 Rudong People's Hospital/Affiliated Rudong Hospital of Xinglin College，斜杠表示同一机构的两种称呼或并列名称，翻译时保留斜杠结构，不拆分为两个地址。


## 联系人识别规则

优先提取通讯作者，通讯作者通常用 * 标注。如果有多位通讯作者，提取排名第一的那位，例如 Jike Song 1,4* 和 Hongsheng Bi 1,2,3,4* 并列通讯作者，则提取排名靠前的 Jike Song。如果论文中没有通讯作者，则提取第一作者，第一作者是作者列表中排名第一的人。如果第一作者有多人（即共同第一作者，用 # 或 † 标注），则提取其中排名第一的那位。如果论文既没有通讯作者标注也没有第一作者信息，或者论文没有任何符号标注，则默认提取作者列表中的第一个人。


## 中国作者识别规则

**A. 姓名判断（中文拼音规则）**：支持复姓。

**B. 地址判断（中国机构）**：地址字段包含关键词 china（不区分大小写）。

**C. 组合判断规则**：同时满足姓名符合中文拼音规则且地址包含中国关键词，视为中国作者。

**D. 排除规则**：地址同时包含 "USA"、"UK"、"Japan" 等其他国家关键词以及 "China"，仍视为中国（如 "China-USA 联合研究所"）。


## 字段对应关系
corresponding_author 下的 address、email、phone 必须对应联系人这个人，不是其他作者的信息。


## 翻译要求

把 address 字段翻译并放到 address_cn 字段。每个完整地址的格式从科室→机构→城市→邮编→国家。所有地址均需翻译，包括非中国机构（如美国、日本等）也需翻译成中文。

示例：
- 四川大学华西医院心内科，成都 610041，中国
- 四川省人民医院心内科，成都 610072，中国

# 论文内容
Title: Investigation of the impact of D, L-methadone on ALA-PDT in multiple glioblastoma cell lines

URL Source: https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7

Markdown Content:
Abstract
--------

### Background

Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM) is the most common and aggressive malignant brain tumor, characterized by infiltrative growth, resistance to chemotherapy and radiotherapy, and an immune-suppressive microenvironment. Interstitial Photodynamic Therapy (iPDT), which involves oral ALA administration and localized light activation, offers a promising approach by killing tumor cells, disrupting tumor vasculature, and stimulating the immune response. However, iPDT faces limitations due to the blood–brain barrier (BBB), insufficient irradiation of satellite and margin tumors, hypoxia within the tumor microenvironment, and glioma cell repair mechanisms. These factors collectively contribute to the suboptimal efficacy of iPDT, emphasizing the need for combined therapeutic strategies to improve outcomes. Some in-vitro research on D, L-methadone, which enhances chemotherapy and ALA-PDT, has caught our interest, although it remains controversial.

### Methods

The levels of the μ-opioid receptor (MOR) were measured in multiple GBM cell lines. Multiple GBM cell lines (A172, U251, GB14) were treated with 2.5 µg/ml and 10 µg/ml D, L-methadone in combination with sublethal-dose ALA-PDT to mimic the limitations of PDT. A MOR antagonist, naloxone (NAL), was used to inhibit the potential role of methadone. Cell viability, apoptosis, PpIX, and ROS were examined.

### Results

The expression level of MOR in GBM cell lines was low. In the A172 cell line, 10 µg/ml methadone enhanced sublethal-dose ALA-PDT-induced apoptosis; however, in the U251 and GB14 cell lines, neither 2.5 µg/ml methadone nor 10 µg/ml methadone could enhance ALA-PDT-induced apoptosis. The viability of the three cell lines was unaffected by methadone combined with ALA-PDT. No reversal effect of naloxone on methadone was observed. Data show that methadone did not significantly enhance PpIX accumulation or ROS generation when combined with ALA-PDT.

### Conclusions

A general sensitizing effect of methadone on ALA-PDT was not observed in this in vitro experiment involving multiple GBM cell lines. The sensitizing effect of methadone on ALA-PDT may depend on the specific cell line used. Therefore, the recommendation to use methadone as a sensitizer for ALA-PDT in the treatment of GBM should be made with caution.

1 Introduction
--------------

Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM), which makes up nearly 50% of malignant brain tumors, remains the most lethal CNS malignancy, with a 5-year survival rate of less than 7% [[1](https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR1 "Ostrom, Q. T., Cote, D. J., Ascha, M., Kruchko, C., & Barnholtz-Sloan, J. S. (2018). Adult glioma incidence and survival by race or ethnicity in the United States from 2000 to 2014. JAMA Oncology, 4(9), 1254–1262.

CBTRUS statistical report: Primary brain and other central nervous system tumors diagnosed in the United States in 2016-2020. Neuro-Oncology, 25(12 Suppl 2), iv1–iv99. 
                https://doi.org/10.1093/neuonc/noad149
                
              "),[3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR3 "Schaff, L. R., & Mellinghoff, I. K. (2023). Glioblastoma and other primary brain malignancies in adults: A review. JAMA, 329(7), 574–587. 
                https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2023.0023
                
              ")]. Despite aggressive multimodal treatment, including maximum surgical resection followed by temozolomide-based chemotherapy and radiation, tumor recurrence occurs in over 90% of patients within 7 months after treatment [[3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR3 "Schaff, L. R., & Mellinghoff, I. K. (2023). Glioblastoma and other primary brain malignancies in adults: A review. JAMA, 329(7), 574–587.

              "), [4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR4 "Fukushima, C. M., & de Groot, J. (2024). Updates for newly diagnosed and recurrent glioblastoma: A review of recent clinical trials. Current Opinion in Neurology, 37(6), 666–671. 
                https://doi.org/10.1097/wco.0000000000001320

Therapeutic failure is mainly attributed to the characteristics of GBM, such as infiltrative growth beyond surgical margins, chemo- and radioresistant stem-like cells, and an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment [[5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR5 "Wu, W., Klockow, J. 5-Aminolevulinic acid-assisted photodynamic therapy (ALA-PDT) has emerged as a promising new treatment for GBM, different from traditional therapies [[7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR7 "Stepp, H., & Stummer, W. This issue compounds the non-uniform light profile produced by the diffuser at short distances from its surface during iPDT, which may have negative consequences for iPDT [[16](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR16 "Stringasci, M. Methadone, a synthetic opioid with analgesic properties, has recently gained attention for its potential antitumor and chemosensitizing effects, although this remains controversial [[18](https://doi.org/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR18 "Friesen, C., Hormann, I., Roscher, M., Fichtner, I., Alt, A., Hilger, R., Debatin, K. J., Thome, C., Brawanski, A., & Proescholdt, M. A. (2018). Efficacy of D, L-methadone in the treatment of glioblastoma in vitro. CNS Oncology, 7(3), Article CNS18.

                https://doi.org/10.3390/biom10060917
                
              ")]. Preclinical studies report conflicting results. In the context of chemotherapy, some studies suggest that methadone enhances chemotherapy-induced apoptosis in leukemia and GBM cells through μ-opioid receptor (MOR)-dependent pathways or by inhibiting P-glycoprotein [[18](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR18 "Friesen, C., Hormann, I., Roscher, M., Fichtner, I., Alt, A., Hilger, R., Debatin, K. M., & Miltner, E. (2014). Opioid receptor activation, triggering downregulation of cAMP, improves the effectiveness of anti-cancer drugs in the treatment of glioblastoma. Cell Cycle, 13(10), 1560–1570. 
                https://doi.org/10.4161/cc.28493
                
              "), [19](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR19 "Brawanski, K., Brockhoff, G., Hau, P., Vollmann-Zwerenz, A., Freyschlag, C., Lohmeier, A., Riemenschneider, M. J., Thome, C., Brawanski, A., & Proescholdt, M. A. (2018). Efficacy of D, L-methadone in the treatment of glioblastoma in vitro. CNS Oncology, 7(3), Article CNS18. 
                https://doi.org/10.2217/cns-2018-0006
                
              ")]. However, more studies demonstrate that there are no synergistic effects between TMZ and radiation in vitro at safe concentrations, and the sensitizing effect observed only at cytotoxic concentrations is drug- and cell line-dependent and independent of the MOR pathway [[20](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR20 "Vatter, T., Klumpp, L., Ganser, K., Stransky, N., Zips, D., Eckert, F., & Huber, S. M. (2020). Against repurposing methadone for glioblastoma therapy. Biomolecules. 
                https://doi.org/10.3390/biom10060917
                
              "), [21](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR21 "Oppermann, H., Matusova, M., Glasow, A., Dietterle, J., Baran-Schmidt, R., Neumann, K., Meixensberger, J., & Gaunitz, F. (2019). D, L-methadone does not improve the efficacy of radio- and chemotherapy in vitro for glioblastoma. Cancer Chemotherapy and Pharmacology, 83(6), 1017–1024. 
                https://doi.org/10.1007/s00280-019-03816-3
                
              ")]. Conversely, new evidence emphasizes the role of methadone in enhancing non-chemotherapeutic treatments. Shi et al. demonstrated that methadone significantly enhanced ALA-PDT efficacy in A172 GBM cells by promoting apoptosis and G0/G1 cell cycle arrest, despite inconsistent effects on PpIX accumulation [[22](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR22 "Shi, L., Buchner, A., Pohla, H., Pongratz, T., Rühm, A., Zimmermann, W., Gederaas, O. A., Zhang, L., Wang, X., Stepp, H., & Sroka, R. (2019). Methadone enhances the effectiveness of 5-aminolevulinic acid-based photodynamic therapy for squamous cell carcinoma and glioblastoma in vitro. Journal of Biophotonics, 12(10), Article e201800468. 
                https://doi.org/10.1002/jbio.201800468
                
              ")]. These context-dependent discrepancies suggest that the sensitizing potential of methadone may critically depend on the therapeutic setting, such as chemotherapy drugs or PDT, and on different cell lines. Due to the heterogeneity of GBM and the conflicting results reported across studies, validation in multiple cell lines is crucial to establish generalizability and clarify the underlying mechanisms. This study aims to investigate further the sensitizing effects of methadone on ALA-PDT across multiple GBM cell lines (A172, U251, U87) using dose–response analyses. Technically, sublethal-dose ALA-PDT in vitro was used to mimic the limitations of PDT, such as limited ALA intake and insufficient light irradiation for GBM tumors, in combination with methadone at two concentrations.

2 Materials and methods
-----------------------

### 2.1 Cell lines and cell culture

GBM cell lines were involved in the experiments: four established cell lines (A172, U251, U87, U373, ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA) and one primary cell line (GB14, LIFE Center, University Hospital, LMU). There are other cell lines: a human chronic myelogenous leukemia cell line, K562, and a human hypopharyngeal squamous cell line, FaDu (ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA). All cell lines were cultured in DMEM (4.5 g glucose/ml, Gibco, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Germany), supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Biochrom, Berlin, Germany), antibiotics (100 U/ml penicillin, 100 μg/ml streptomycin, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany), and MEM non-essential amino acids (Gibco, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Germany). The cells were cultivated in an incubator at 37˚C with 5% CO 2 and passaged with 0.05% trypsin/0.02% EDTA every 2–3 days. The cells were seeded in 96-well plates at densities of 5 × 10 3, 2.5 × 10 3, and 5 × 10 3 cells per well (A172, U251, GB14) and in 6-well plates at densities of 5 × 10 5, 2.5 × 10 5, and 5 × 10 5 cells per well (A172, U251, GB14).

### 2.2 Measurement of the μ-opioid receptor (MOR) in GBM cell lines

GBM cell lines (A172, U87, U251, U373) and two positive control cell lines (K562 and FADU) were included in this MOR assay. Cells were harvested into flow cytometry tubes. After two washes with 1%bovine serum albumin(BSA) in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), BSA/PBS solution, the cells were resuspended in 100 µl BSA/PBS solution and incubated for 20 min, and then treated with 5 µl OPRM1 Polyclonal Antibody (PA5-26138, Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, USA) for 1 h. After washing with BSA/PBS solution, the cells were resuspended in 250 µl BSA/PBS solution and incubated with 1 µl Goat anti-Rabbit IgG Cross-Adsorbed Secondary Antibody (FITC conjugate) (F-2765, Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, USA) for 1 h. Following a final wash with BSA/PBS, MOR levels were measured using a flow cytometer (FACS Calibur, BD, Heidelberg, Germany). Three groups were included for each cell line: cells only group, cells + Secondary Antibody/FITC (control group), and cells + antibody + Secondary Antibody/FITC (experiment group). All cell treatments were performed on ice in a dark room. Fluorescence was excited at 488 nm, detected with a 530 nm filter, and measured by the FL1 photomultiplier tube of the flow cytometer. The mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) was calculated using the software FlowJo v10 Workspace (FlowJo, LLC, OR, USA). Relative fluorescence intensity (RFI) represents the fold change in MOR levels. RFI = (MFI of experimental group—MFI of cells only group) / (MFI of control group—MFI of cells only group).

### 2.3 Cytotoxicity measurement of methadone

D, L-methadone hydrochloride powder (M0267, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) was dissolved in 96% ethanol and aliquoted for storage at room temperature. The stock solution (5 ~ 40 μg/ml) with DMEM ± FBS (complete medium, CM, or serum-free medium, SFM) was stored at 4℃ for less than 1 week and wrapped in aluminum foil. The cells were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated with methadone at various concentrations (final concentrations of 2.5, 5, 10, and 20 μg/ml). The medium was renewed after 48 h with SFM, consistent with the apoptosis procedure. After 72 h, cytotoxicity was assessed using the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8, 96,992, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) on the ELISA Microplate Reader (450 nm, Molecular Devices, San Jose, CA, USA).

### 2.4 Sublethal-dose ALA-PDT parameters

According to previous pre-experiments [[22](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR22 "Shi, L., Buchner, A., Pohla, H., Pongratz, T., Rühm, A., Zimmermann, W., Gederaas, O. A., Zhang, L., Wang, X., Stepp, H., & Sroka, R. (2019). Methadone enhances the effectiveness of 5-aminolevulinic acid-based photodynamic therapy for squamous cell carcinoma and glioblastoma in vitro. Journal of Biophotonics, 12(10), Article e201800468. 
                https://doi.org/10.1002/jbio.201800468
                
              ")], the sublethal-dose ALA-PDT was defined as a 20%-40% reduction in viability 24 h post-PDT. The sublethal-dose ALA-PDT parameter varies depending on cell lines (A172, 1 mM ALA, 100 mW/cm 2, 1.2 J/cm 2; U251, 0.5 mM ALA, 100 mW/cm 2, 1 J/cm 2; GB14, 0.5 mM ALA, 100 mW/cm 2, 0.5 J/cm 2). The ALA-PDT parameter was utilized throughout the experiment. ALA (number, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) was dissolved in SFM for 24 h of incubation with cells. ALA solutions were freshly prepared each time. Cells were irradiated with a laser irradiation chamber (600 μm fiber with custom-made projection optics connected to a 635 nm Ceralas diode laser, CeramOptec GmbH, Bonn, Germany) [[23](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#ref-CR23 "Knappe, A., Beyer, W., Riesenberg, R., Schneede, P., Sroka, R., Unsöld, E., & Valet, G. (1995). [Computer-controlled laser irradiation unit for studies of light-induced processes in cell cultures]. Biomedical Engineering = Biomedizinische Technik, 40(10), 272–275. 
                https://doi.org/10.1515/bmte.1995.40.10.272
                
              . (Computergesteuerte Laserbestrahlungseinheit für Untersuchungen lichtinduzierter Prozesse an Zellkulturen.).")].

### 2.5 Cell viability assay

Six groups were included in the cell viability assay: the untreated cells group (UC), the methadone 10 µg/ml only group (MET10), the ALA-PDT group (PDT), the methadone plus ALA-PDT group (PDT + MET 2.5 µg/ml, PDT + MET 10 µg/mL), and the ALA-PDT plus methadone 10 µg/ml plus naloxone 0.0213 µg/ml (0.065 μM) (PDT + MET 10 + NAL). The cells were seeded in 96-well plates. After 48 h, the medium was replaced with SFM, and ALA was incubated for an additional 24 h before irradiation, as described above (paragraph 2.4). Following irradiation, the SFM was renewed immediately to complete the medium. After another 24 h, cell viability was assessed using CCK8 (as paragraph 2.3).

### 2.6 Apoptosis assay

Cell apoptosis was assessed using an Annexin V-APC/7-AAD apoptosis detection kit (BD Biosciences, Germany) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Six groups were included in the apoptosis assay, as in the cell viability assay. The cells were seeded in 6-well plates. The cells were collected into flow cytometry tubes 24 h after irradiation, washed twice with 2 ml PBS, and resuspended in 100 μl of Annexin V buffer containing 5 μl Annexin V-APC and 5 μl 7-AAD for 15 min on ice in the dark. Following this, 100 μl of 1 × BD buffer was added before the assay. The apoptosis rate was determined using flow cytometry (FACS Calibur, BD, Heidelberg, Germany). Annexin V-APC and 7-AAD fluorescence were excited at 633 nm and 488 nm, respectively, with detection at 670 nm. The cell populations were analyzed as follows: viable cells (Annexin V-APC-/7-AAD-), early apoptotic cells (Annexin V-APC + /7-AAD-), and late apoptotic/necrotic cells (Annexin V-APC + /7-AAD +). The total apoptotic cell percentage was the sum of the early- and late-apoptotic populations. All apoptosis experiments were conducted in triplicate across five independent assays.

### 2.7 PpIX fluorescence assay after methadone incubation with ALA

Four groups were designed for PpIX assays: the untreated cells group (UC), the methadone-only group (MET), the ALA-only group (ALA), and the methadone plus ALA group (MET + ALA). The cell lines were seeded in 6-well plates with or without methadone (10 μg/ml) for 72 h. After 48 h of incubation, the SFM was renewed in parallel with or without ALA (ALA dose is consistent with the sublethal-dose ALA-PDT parameter in this experimental system, A172, 1 mM; U251, 0.5 mM; GB14, 1 mM). PpIX fluorescence was measured using flow cytometry (FACS Calibur, BD, Germany) at an excitation wavelength of 633 nm with detection at a wavelength of 670 nm. The Geometric Mean Fluorescence Intensity (MFI) of PpIX fluorescence was calculated using FlowJo v10 workspace (FlowJo LLC, OR, USA). All PpIX experiments were performed in triplicate across three independent assays.

### 2.8 Reactive oxygen species (ROS) assay

Four groups were designed in the ROS assay: the untreated cells group (UC), the methadone only group (MET), the ALA-PDT-only group (PDT), and the methadone plus ALA-PDT group (MET + PDT). The intracellular ROS generation was measured using the DCFDA Cellular ROS Detection Assay Kit (ab113851, Abcam, UK). DCFDA is a fluorogenic dye that detects hydroxyl, peroxyl, and other ROS activities within cells. When ROS are present, this compound is oxidized within cells, producing the fluorescent compound 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF), which remains within cells. The cell lines were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated with methadone (10 μg/ml). After 48 h, the SFM was renewed, and ALA was incubated for an additional 24 h. Twenty micromolar DCFDA was added to the wells 10 min before irradiation. After irradiation, cells were incubated in medium containing 20 μM DCFDA for 90 min at 37°C in an incubator. The ROS fluorescence was then assayed using a microplate reader (Fluostar Optima, BMG LabTech, Ortenberg, Germany) at an excitation wavelength of 685 nm, with detection at 535 nm. All experiments were performed in triplicate across three independent assays (see Fig.[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig1)).

### 2.9 Statistical method

Each assay was performed in 3 to 5 duplicates, and three independent assays were conducted (n = 3 or 5). Statistical tests were conducted using SPSS 13.0 software (IBM Corporation, Armonk, NY, USA). The One-way ANOVA Test was used to compare the survival rates, PpIX fluorescence, apoptosis rates, and ROS between different groups. P-values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

3 Results
---------

### 3.1 MOR expression of GBM cell lines

The relative fluorescence intensity (RFI) of MOR in K562, FADU (positive control cell lines), A172, U87, U251, and U373 cell lines was evaluated to investigate the expression levels of MOR in various cell lines (Fig.[2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig2)). In the K562 cell line, the RFI of MOR expression was 6.77 ± 0.39, significantly higher compared to the other cell lines (p < 0.0001). In the FADU cell line, the RFI of MOR expression was 4.56 ± 0.21, also substantially higher than the other cell lines (all p < 0.0001). In the A172 cell line, the RFI of MOR expression was 1.22 ± 0.01, which was significantly lower compared to the K562 and FADU cell lines (p < 0.0001). In the U251 cell line, the RFI of MOR expression was 1.54 ± 0.35, significantly lower than that of the K562 and FADU cell lines (p < 0.0001), but not substantially different from the A172 cell line (p = 0.2296). In the U87 cell line, the RFI of MOR expression was 1.46 ± 0.28, considerably lower than that of the K562 and FADU cell lines (p < 0.0001), but not significantly different from the A172 cell line and U251 cell lines (U87 vs A172, p = 0.8569; U87 vs U251, p = 0.8503). In the U373 cell line, the RFI of MOR expression was 1.68 ± 0.19, considerably lower than that of the K562 and FADU cell lines (p < 0.0001), but not significantly different from the A172, U251, and U87 cell lines (U373 vs A172, p = 0.0511; U373 vs U251, p = 0.9693; U373 vs U87, p = 0.4062;).

### 3.2 Low cytotoxicity of methadone on the three GBM cell lines

To investigate the cytotoxicity of methadone on glioblastoma cell lines, the cell viability of A172, U251, and GB14 after incubation with gradient concentrations of methadone (2.5, 5, 10, 20 μg/mL) for 72 h was evaluated using the CCK8 assay (experimental schedule details shown in Fig.[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig1)). In the A172 cell line, the cell viability (%) of methadone-treated groups at gradient concentrations was 96.05 ± 3.95, 93.39 ± 6.61, 93.74 ± 6.26, and 88.12 ± 11.88, compared to the untreated A172 cells (UC: untretad control) group, respectively (MET 2.5 vs UC, p = 0.8500; MET 5 vs UC, p = 0.2897, MET 10 vs UC, p = 0.0866; MET 20 vs UC, p < 0.0001). For the U251 cell line, the cell viability (%) of methadone groups was 106.80 ± 6.61, 109.20 ± 7.16, 105.60 ± 4.04, and 102.20 ± 6.61, compared to the U251 UC group, respectively (MET 2.5 vs UC, p = 0.2445; MET 5 vs UC, p = 0.0649, MET 10 vs UC, p = 0.4201; MET 20 vs UC, p = 0.9549). In the GB14 cell line, the cell viability (%) was 103.78 ± 9.76, 98.66 ± 7.08, 97.36 ± 8.07, and 97.30 ± 10.67, compared to the GB14 UC group, respectively (MET 2.5 vs UC, p = 0.9868; MET 5 vs UC, p = 0.9743, MET 10 vs UC, p = 0.8696; MET 20 vs UC, p = 0.9372). U251 and GB14 all exhibited robust viability even at a high methadone concentration (20 μg/mL). In general, methadone displayed low cytotoxicity toward the three GBM cell lines. Based on the results and our previous experiments, two methadone concentrations (2.5, 10 μg/mL) were selected for subsequent experiments (see Fig.[3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig3)).

### 3.3 Methadone effects on the viability of GBM cell lines caused by sublethal-dose ALA-PDT

To further determine the impact on the viability of methadone on sublethal-dose PDT-induced, two concentrations (2.5, 10 μg/ml) of methadone were used on a sublethal-dose ALA-PDT basis.

In the A172 cell line, the cell viability (%) of five groups (MET, PDT, PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, PDT + MET10 + NAL) is 92.00 ± 5.43, 47.60 ± 10.31, 58.00 ± 15.60, 46.20 ± 8.70, and 51.80 ± 21.42, respectively, compared to the A172 UC group (MET vs UC, p = 0.0616; PDT vs UC, p = 0.0011; PDT + MET 2.5 vs UC, p = 0.0122; PDT + MET 10 vs UC, p = 0.0122; PDT + MET10 + NAL vs UC, p = 0.0180, all p < 0.05, except the MET group). There is no statistical significance among the PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, and PDT + MET10 + NAL groups when compared to the PDT group (PDT vs PDT + MET 2.5, p = 0.7015; PDT vs PDT + MET 10, p = 0.9927; PDT vs PDT + MET 10 + NAL, p = 0.9113), indicating that methadone does not affect the viability of A172 after sublethal-dose PDT.

In the U251 cell line, the cell viability (%) of five groups (MET, PDT, PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, PDT + MET10 + NAL) is 103 ± 13.70, 74.58 ± 2.61, 83.09 ± 6.47, 87.78 ± 8.17, and 68.97 ± 7.15, respectively, compared to the U251 UC group (all p < 0.05, except the MET group). There is no statistical significance between the PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, and PDT + MET10 + NAL groups compared to the PDT group (PDT vs PDT + MET 2.5, p = 0.5462; PDT vs PDT + MET 10, p = 0.1922; PDT vs PDT + MET 10 + NAL, p = 0.8359), indicating that methadone does not have an impact on the viability of U251 after sublethal-dose PDT.

In the GB14 cell line, the cell viability (%) of 5 groups (MET, PDT, PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, PDT + MET10 + NAL) is 87 ± 9.14, 61.20 ± 16.08, 70 ± 10.32, 55.40 ± 14.74, 58.00 ± 9.77 compared to the GB14 UC group (all p < 0.05, except MET group). This indicates that PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, and PDT + MET10 + NAL groups are not statistically significant compared to the PDT group (PDT vs PDT + MET 2.5, p = 0.6734; PDT vs PDT + MET 10, p = 0.8882; PDT vs PDT + MET 10 + NAL, p = 0.9108), which means that methadone doesn’t affect the viability of GB14 after sublethal-dose PDT (see Fig.[4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig4)).

### 3.4 Impact of methadone on the cell death pathway

Apoptosis has been recognized as the primary mode of cell death resulting from sublethal-dose ALA-PDT. To determine whether methadone affects the PDT-induced cell death pathway, e.g., apoptosis, an apoptosis assay using annexin V-APC and 7-AAD was performed. Six groups were explored in the apoptosis assay (UC, MET10, PDT, PDT + MET2.5, PDT + MET10, PDT + MET10 + NAL).

In the A172 cell lines, 24 h after irradiation, the proportions of apoptosis (%) were 4.39 ± 1.10, 5.27 ± 1.65, 18.69 ± 4.68, 35.55 ± 14.44, 54.36 ± 12.77, and 45.53 ± 15.23 for the six groups, respectively. It seems that 2.5 μg/mL methadone tends to improve PDT-induced apoptosis, but the difference is not significant (p = 0.9762). However, 10 μg/mL methadone significantly enhances PDT-induced apoptosis with a statistically significant difference (p = 0.0208).

In the U251 cell lines, 24 h after irradiation, the proportions of apoptosis (%) were 3.56 ± 2.96, 5.09 ± 3.08, 27.39 ± 6.31, 29.66 ± 6.42, 29.34 ± 11.58, and 27.04 ± 10.17 for the six groups, respectively. Methadone did not significantly improve PDT-induced apoptosis (PDT vs PDT + MET 2.5, p = 0.9965; PDT vs PDT + MET 10, p = 0.9983; PDT vs PDT + MET 10 + NAL, p > 0.9999, all p > 0.05).

In the GB14 cell lines, 24 h after irradiation, the proportions of apoptosis (%) were 4.99 ± 2.56, 11.22 ± 7.42, 33.13 ± 4.74, 30.57 ± 9.48, 30.42 ± 13.34, and 27.69 ± 11.75 for the six groups, respectively. Methadone did not significantly enhance PDT-induced apoptosis (PDT vs PDT + MET 2.5, p = 0.4797; PDT vs PDT + MET 10, p = 0.7871; PDT vs PDT + MET 10 + NAL, p = 0.7451, all p > 0.05).

Overall, these results showed that methadone enhanced sublethal-dose PDT-induced apoptosis in a cell line-dependent manner (see Fig.[5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig5)).

### 3.5 Impact of methadone on PpIX formation and ROS generation in GBM cell lines

To obtain an in-depth understanding of methadone’s role in promoting PDT-induced apoptosis, it is necessary first to determine whether methadone affects the production of PpIX from ALA during the photophysical process and the generation of ROS during the photochemical reaction.

In the A172 cell line, the PpIX fluorescence values (mean ± SD, a.u.) for four groups—the untreated cells group (UC), the methadone only group (MET), the ALA only group (ALA), and the ALA plus methadone group (ALA + MET) are 15.67 ± 3.38, 21.33 ± 10.40, 346.90 ± 118.60, and 343.6 ± 106, respectively. Methadone neither generated PpIX in A172 (MET vs UC, p > 0.9999) nor promoted ALA-derived PpIX (ALA vs ALA + MET, p > 0.9999). In the U251 cell line, the PpIX fluorescence values for the UC, MET, ALA, and ALA + MET groups are 2.12 ± 0.10, 5.43 ± 1.00, 251.4 ± 43.08, and 192.7 ± 27.15, respectively. Methadone did not produce PpIX in U251 (MET vs UC, p > 0.9999) or enhance ALA-derived PpIX (ALA vs ALA + MET, p = 0.1264). In the GB14 cell line, the PpIX fluorescence values for the UC, MET, ALA, and ALA + MET groups are 19.28 ± 4.96, 22.85 ± 2.18, 416.30 ± 101.60, and 434.80 ± 134.10, respectively. Methadone did not generate PpIX in GB14 (MET vs UC, p = 0.9994) or promote ALA-derived PpIX (ALA vs ALA + MET, p = 0.9340).

Overall, in this experimental system, methadone did not generate PpIX in the three GBM cell lines or enhance ALA-derived PpIX (Fig.[6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43630-026-00863-7#Fig6)A, B).
