# 论文内容
Title: Peritumoural adipose tissue promotes ferroptosis resistance by 3-hydroxykynurenine-mediated suppression of ferritinophagy

URL Source: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41556-026-01907-x

Markdown Content:
Main
----

The tumour microenvironment (TME) is dynamically shaped by adjacent stromal components[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR1 "Haynes, N. M., Chadwick, T. B. & Parker, B. S. The complexity of immune evasion mechanisms throughout the metastatic cascade. Nat. Immunol. 25, 1793–1808 (2024)."). Among these, peritumoural adipose tissue (PAT) is anatomically associated with many malignancies, including breast cancer, renal cell carcinoma and abdominal tumours such as gastric cancer (GC), colorectal cancer (CRC) and ovarian cancer[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR2 "Brown, K. A. & Scherer, P. E. Update on adipose tissue and cancer. Endocr. Rev. 44, 961–974 (2023)."). Clinically, PAT correlates with poor prognosis in various cancers[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR3 "Quail, D. F. & Dannenberg, A. J. The obese adipose tissue microenvironment in cancer development and progression. Nat. Rev. Endocrinol. 15, 139–154 (2019).") and serves as an independent predictor of recurrence-free survival in CRC[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR4 "Ahn, H. et al. Prognostic significance of imaging features of peritumoral adipose tissue in FDG PET/CT of patients with colorectal cancer. Eur. J. Radiol. 145, 110047 (2021)."). Adipocytes promote tumour proliferation through metabolic coupling, primarily by supplying free fatty acids and lactate to fuel tumour cell metabolism[5](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR5 "Nieman, K. M. et al. Adipocytes promote ovarian cancer metastasis and provide energy for rapid tumor growth. Nat. Med. 17, 1498–1503 (2011)."),[6](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR6 "Wei, G. et al. The thermogenic activity of adjacent adipocytes fuels the progression of ccRCC and compromises anti-tumor therapeutic efficacy. Cell Metab. 33, 2021–2039 (2021)."),[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR7 "Dong, K. et al. Metabolic crosstalk between thermogenic adipocyte and cancer cell: dysfunction and therapeutics. Curr. Opin. Pharmacol. 68, 102322 (2023)."). In parallel, adipocytes also upregulate CD36 in cancer cells to enhance free fatty acid uptake and secrete adipokines to facilitate invasion and migration[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR8 "Ladanyi, A. et al. Adipocyte-induced CD36 expression drives ovarian cancer progression and metastasis. Oncogene 37, 2285–2301 (2018)."),[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR9 "Di Franco, S. et al. Adipose stem cell niche reprograms the colorectal cancer stem cell metastatic machinery. Nat. Commun. 12, 5006 (2021)."). Despite these advances, the precise mechanisms by which PAT drives tumour progression and therapeutic resistance remain incompletely understood.

Ferroptosis is a regulated cell death driven by iron-dependent lipid peroxidation, making intracellular iron pivotal for its sensitivity[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR10 "Jiang, X., Stockwell, B. R. & Conrad, M. Ferroptosis: mechanisms, biology and role in disease. Nat. Rev. Mol. Cell Biol. 22, 266–282 (2021)."). Ferritin, composed of ferritin heavy chain (FTH1) and ferritin light chain (FTL) subunits, maintains iron homeostasis by sequestering or releasing Fe 2+ (ref. [11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR11 "Zhang, Y. Y., Han, Y., Li, W. N., Xu, R. H. & Ju, H. Q. Tumor iron homeostasis and immune regulation. Trends Pharmacol. Sci. 45, 145–156 (2024).")). This process is linked to ferroptosis through ferritinophagy, in which nuclear receptor coactivator 4 (NCOA4) binds ferritin to degrade it and release stored iron, promoting lipid peroxidation[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR12 "Galy, B., Conrad, M. & Muckenthaler, M. Mechanisms controlling cellular and systemic iron homeostasis. Nat. Rev. Mol. Cell Biol. 25, 133–155 (2024)."). Previous studies revealed that activation and diffusion of lysosomal iron initiates membrane lipid oxidation and ferroptosis[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR13 "Cañeque, T. et al. Activation of lysosomal iron triggers ferroptosis in cancer. Nature 642, 492–500 (2025)."),[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR14 "Saimoto, Y. et al. Lysosomal lipid peroxidation contributes to ferroptosis induction via lysosomal membrane permeabilization. Nat. Commun. 16, 3554 (2025)."), and evidence links ferritinophagy to radiation-induced ferroptosis[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR15 "Zhou, H. et al. NCOA4-mediated ferritinophagy is involved in ionizing radiation-induced ferroptosis of intestinal epithelial cells. Redox Biol. 55, 102413 (2022)."), thus highlighting its critical role in regulating ferroptosis. Conversely, suppression of ferritinophagy impairs iron release and confers ferroptosis resistance[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR16 "Li, K. et al. TRIM7 modulates NCOA4-mediated ferritinophagy and ferroptosis in glioblastoma cells. Redox Biol. 56, 102451 (2022)."),[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR17 "Liu, Z. et al. E3 ubiquitin ligase DTX2 fosters ferroptosis resistance via suppressing NCOA4-mediated ferritinophagy in non-small cell lung cancer. Drug Resist. Updat. 77, 101154 (2024)."). Pharmacologically disrupting the NCOA4–FTH1 interaction reduces ferrous iron levels and ferroptosis susceptibility[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR18 "Fang, Y. et al. Inhibiting ferroptosis through disrupting the NCOA4–FTH1 interaction: a new mechanism of action. ACS Cent Sci 7, 980–989 (2021)."). Emerging evidence has established ferroptosis as a distinct form of immunogenic cell death that critically regulates CD8+ T cell-mediated antitumour immunity and immunotherapy[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR19 "Efimova, I. et al. Vaccination with early ferroptotic cancer cells induces efficient antitumor immunity. J. Immunother. Cancer 8, e001369 (2020)."),[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR20 "Bell, H. N., Stockwell, B. R. & Zou, W. Ironing out the role of ferroptosis in immunity. Immunity 57, 941–956 (2024)."). Our previous work also demonstrated that interleukin-1β within the TME protects tumour cells from ferroptosis by sustaining iron homeostasis[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR21 "Han, Y. et al. IL-1β-associated NNT acetylation orchestrates iron–sulfur cluster maintenance and cancer immunotherapy resistance. Mol. Cell 83, 1887–1902 (2023)."). However, the role of PAT in modulating ferroptosis remains unknown.

In this study, we aimed to elucidate the role of PAT in modulating ferroptosis susceptibility in cancer cells. We found that PAT elevates 3-hydroxykynurenine (3-HK) in cancer cells, which directly binds NCOA4 to suppress ferritinophagy and confer ferroptosis resistance. Furthermore, inhibiting the kynurenine (KYN) pathway synergized with anti-PD-1 therapy to achieve robust tumour eradication in adipose-rich tumour models. This study reveals a PAT-driven metabolic mechanism of ferroptosis resistance and proposes a dual-targeted strategy combining KYN pathway inhibition with immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) to enhance immunotherapy in PAT-associated tumours.

Results
-------

### PAT confers tumour ferroptosis resistance and reduces the immunotherapy efficacy

In advanced CRC and GC, tumours frequently invade through the serosa and directly contact visceral adipose tissue (Fig. [1a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig1)). To investigate whether PAT affects ferroptosis, we established two in vivo models by implanting CRC tumours either into the mid-dorsal subcutaneous region (a non-adipose site) or into the inguinal white adipose tissue (iWAT, an adipose-associated site), as described in a previous report[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR22 "Iwamoto, H. et al. Cancer lipid metabolism confers antiangiogenic drug resistance. Cell Metab. 28, 104–117 (2018).") (Fig. [1b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig1)). First, using doxycycline-inducible knockdown of the key ferroptosis suppressor glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4-iKD)[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR23 "Yang, W. S. et al. Regulation of ferroptotic cancer cell death by GPX4. Cell 156, 317–331 (2014)."), we found iWAT-implanted DLD1 xenografts were more resistant to ferroptosis than subcutaneous tumours (Fig. [1c,d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig1) and Extended Data Fig. [1a–c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig8)). Immunohistochemistry (IHC) confirmed lower lipid peroxidation (4-hydroxynonenal (4-HNE) and malondialdehyde (MDA)) in iWAT-associated tumours after GPX4 silencing (Fig. [1e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig1) and Extended Data Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig8)). Similar resistance occurred in MC38 tumours with inducible knockdown of the cystine transporter SLC7A11[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR24 "Koppula, P., Zhuang, L. & Gan, B. Cystine transporter SLC7A11/xCT in cancer: ferroptosis, nutrient dependency, and cancer therapy. Protein Cell 12, 599–620 (2021).") (Extended Data Fig. [1e–g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig8)).

**Fig. 1: PAT confers tumour ferroptosis resistance and reduces immunotherapy efficacy.**

[![Image 1: Fig. 1: PAT confers tumour ferroptosis resistance and reduces immunotherapy efficacy.](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41556-026-01907-x/MediaObjects/41556_2026_1907_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x/figures/1)

**a**, Representative images and haematoxylin–eosin (HE) staining of surgical specimens from patients with GC and those with CRC (scale bars, 400 μm or 100 μm). **b**, Representative images and HE staining of tumours from non-adipose and WAT-implanted tumour models (scale bars, 400 μm or 100 μm). **c**–**e**, Growth curves, tumour weights (**c**), tumour inhibition rates (**d**) and IHC staining scores of 4-HNE (**e**) in BALB/c nude mice implanted with GPX4-knockdown or control (Ctrl) DLD1 cells at non-adipose or WAT-enriched sites (_n_ = 6 mice per group). **f**–**h**, Growth curves, tumour weights (**f**), tumour inhibition rates (**g**) and IHC staining scores of 4-HNE (**h**) in C57BL/6J mice bearing MC38 tumours implanted at non-adipose or WAT-enriched sites after treatment with IKE (_n_ = 6 mice per group). **i**–**k**, Growth curves, tumour weights (**i**), tumour inhibition rates (**j**) and IHC staining scores of 4-HNE (**k**) in C57BL/6J mice bearing MC38 tumours at non-adipose or WAT-enriched sites after treatment with anti-PD-1 antibody or IgG (_n_ = 6 mice per group). **l**,**m**, Representative IHC images (scale bars, 200 μm or 50 μm) and staining scores of 4-HNE (**l**) and MDA (**m**) in samples from patients with CRC. The data are presented as a box-and-whisker graph (bounds of box: first to third quartile, bottom and top line: minimum to maximum, central line: median). Samples are stratified by American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) 8th edition pathological stage (stage I, _n_ = 23; stage III–IV, _n_ = 163). Data and error bars are presented as mean ± s.d. _P_ values were calculated using two-way ANOVA, one-way ANOVA or two-tailed, unpaired Student’s _t_-test.

[Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#MOESM9)

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x/figures/1)

To validate the generality of PAT-mediated ferroptosis resistance, we then assessed the tumour response under pharmacological and immunological stress. In C57BL/6J mice with MC38 tumours, iWAT-implanted tumours showed markedly reduced sensitivity to the ferroptosis inducer imidazole ketone erastin (IKE), with tumour growth inhibition declining from 82.5% in subcutaneous implants to 0.24% in PAT-associated tumours (Fig. [1f,g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig1) and Extended Data Fig. [1i](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig8)). This IKE effect was confirmed as ferroptosis dependent, as it was rescued by Liproxstatin-1 (Lip-1) in subcutaneous tumours (Extended Data Fig. [1h](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig8)). Accordingly, IHC revealed diminished lipid peroxidation in IKE-treated iWAT tumours (Fig. [1h](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig1) and Extended Data Fig. [1j](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig8)). Interestingly, PAT promoted tumour growth only in immunocompetent C57BL/6J mice, not in immunodeficient BALB/c nude mice (Fig. [1c,f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig1)). This pro-tumour effect was abolished upon CD8+ T cell depletion (Extended Data Fig. [2a,b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig9)). Consistently, in Rag1−/− mice, tumour growth was similar at non-adipose and iWAT sites, but adoptive transfer of CD8+ T cells selectively inhibited tumours at the non-adipose site (Extended Data Fig. [2c,d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig9)). Thus, PAT protects tumours from both exogenous ferroptosis inducers and endogenous CD8+ T cell-mediated ferroptotic stress.

Given that CD8+ T cell-derived interferon-γ sensitizes tumour cells to ferroptosis during ICB, we additionally compared the efficacy of anti-PD-1 therapy between different implantation sites. In line with our previous observations, iWAT-implanted CRC tumours exhibited reduced response to anti-PD-1 treatment compared with subcutaneous tumours (Fig. [1i,j](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig1) and Extended Data Fig. [2e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig9)), accompanied by diminished lipid peroxidation (Fig. [1k](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig1) and Extended Data Fig. [2f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig9)). Consistent with the adipose-embedded status of serosa-invading tumours, lipid peroxidation signals (4-HNE/MDA) were significantly attenuated in stage III–IV tumours compared with stage I tumours that remain anatomically segregated from adipose compartments (Fig. [1l,m](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig1) and Extended Data Fig. [2g,h](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig9)) Collectively, these results demonstrate that PAT confers resistance to ferroptosis in cancer cells, thereby impairing antitumour immunity and reducing the efficacy of immune checkpoint therapy.

### PAT confers tumour ferroptosis resistance via ferritin-mediated iron sequestration

Because PAT mediates ferroptosis resistance in tumours, we speculated that PAT secretes protective factors. To test this hypothesis, we established an adipose-derived conditioned media (adipose-CM) assay for in vitro validation (Fig. [2a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig2)). Adipose-CM significantly attenuated RAS-selective lethal 3 (RSL-3)-induced lipid peroxidation and ferroptosis in human CRC cells (Fig. [2b,c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig2) and Extended Data Fig. [3a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig10)) and murine MC38 cells (Extended Data Fig. [3b,c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig10)). Although antioxidants such as vitamin E and extra-mitochondrial coenzyme Q10 (CoQ10) can suppress ferroptosis[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR25 "Calhoon, D. et al. Glycosaminoglycan-driven lipoprotein uptake protects tumours from ferroptosis. Nature 644, 799–808 (2025)."),[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR26 "Bersuker, K. et al. The CoQ oxidoreductase FSP1 acts parallel to GPX4 to inhibit ferroptosis. Nature 575, 688–692 (2019)."), their levels were unchanged in adipose-associated tumours (Extended Data Fig. [3d,e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig10)). To systematically elucidate the mechanism by which PAT contributes to ferroptosis resistance, we conducted quantitative proteomic analysis and found that tumour cells cultured with adipose-CM exhibited distinct proteomic signatures compared with controls (Fig. [2d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig2) and Supplementary Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#MOESM2)). Notably, both FTH1 and FTL were markedly upregulated in adipose-CM-treated cells (Fig. [2e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig2)), while other core ferroptosis regulators remained unchanged (Extended Data Fig. [3f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig10)). Immunoblotting confirmed selective induction of FTH1/FTL without altering other iron-related proteins (Fig. [2f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig2)) or intracellular glutathione (GSH) levels (Extended Data Fig. [3g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig10)), indicating a GSH-independent mechanism centred on ferritin upregulation.

Ferritin is typically upregulated by iron supplementation. However, FerroOrange staining showed reduced intracellular Fe 2+ in adipose-CM-treated cells (Fig. [2g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig2) and Extended Data Fig. [3h](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig10)), indicating the ferritin increase was not due to iron accumulation. Dual knockdown of FTH1 and FTL attenuated this Fe 2+ depletion and abolished the ferroptosis protective effect of adipose-CM against RSL-3 in DLD1 and HCT116 cells (Fig. [2h–j](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig2) and Extended Data Fig. [3i–l](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig10)), indicating that ferritin is essential for adipose-CM-mediated cytoprotection.

To validate this in vivo, we established an mCherry-labelled MC38 tumour model and performed subsequent analyses (Extended Data Fig. [3m](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig10)). MC38 tumour cells from adipose-rich inguinal sites showed higher FTH1/FTL expression, lower intracellular Fe 2+, reduced lipid peroxidation and greater ferroptosis resistance compared with subcutaneous tumours (Fig. [2k–n](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig2)). The peritoneum is an adipose-rich environment and a common site for gastrointestinal metastasis[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR27 "Mukherjee, A., Bilecz, A. J. & Lengyel, E. The adipocyte microenvironment and cancer. Cancer Metastasis Rev. 41, 575–587 (2022)."),[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR28 "Mukherjee, A. et al. Adipocytes reprogram cancer cell metabolism by diverting glucose towards glycerol-3-phosphate thereby promoting metastasis. Nat. Metab. 5, 1563–1577 (2023)."). We therefore established a peritoneal tumour model by intraperitoneally injecting MC38 cells into C57BL/6J mice. These tumours showed higher FTH1/FTL expression than subcutaneous tumours (Fig. [2o](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig2)). Notably, FTH1/FTL knockdown inhibited peritoneal tumour progression, as indicated by decreased tumour mass and burden (Fig. [2p,q](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig2)), confirming the pathophysiological relevance of this axis in promoting peritoneal metastasis. Collectively, these results demonstrate that PAT confers ferroptosis resistance by upregulating ferritin to sequester intracellular iron.

### PAT establishes a KYN-rich TME to elevate ferritin in cancer cells

Building on evidence that PAT-derived factors induce ferritin-mediated iron sequestration, we further characterized the components responsible for this effect. The activity of adipose-CM to upregulate FTH1/FTL was heat-stable and remained after 3-kDa filtration (Fig. [3a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig3)), pointing to low-molecular-weight metabolites as the active factor. Ferritin subunits decrease with adipose-CM dilution, confirming this induction by concentrated metabolites (Extended Data Fig. [3n](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig10)). Metabolomic profiling identified KYN as the only metabolite consistently elevated in both adipose-CM and iWAT-implanted MC38 tumours (Fig. [3b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig3) and Supplementary Table [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#MOESM3)). Notably, conditioned medium from PAT of patients with CRC and MC38 tumour-bearing mice contained 124 μM and 97 μM KYN, respectively (Supplementary Table [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#MOESM4)). As expected, KYN dose-dependently increased FTH1/FTL, decreased intracellular Fe 2+ and suppressed RSL-3-induced lipid peroxidation and ferroptosis in cancer cells (Fig. [3c–f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig3)).

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x/figures/3)

KYN, a tryptophan (TRP) metabolite whose production is limited by indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase 1 (IDO1)/tryptophan 2,3-dioxygenase 2 (TDO2)[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR29 "Luke, J. J. et al. Phase 1/2 study of the indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase 1 inhibitor linrodostat mesylate combined with nivolumab or nivolumab and ipilimumab in advanced solid tumors or hematologic malignancies. Clin. Cancer Res. 31, 2134–2144 (2025)."), led us to hypothesize that PAT creates a KYN-rich microenvironment to promote ferroptosis resistance. Targeted metabolomics of tumour interstitial fluid (TIF) confirmed this, showing elevated KYN (up to 50 µM) and depleted TRP in TIF from PAT-associated tumours versus subcutaneous tumours (Fig. [3g–i](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig3)). In vitro, adipose-CM also increased intracellular KYN (Fig. [3j,k](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig3)). We next evaluated potential transporters (SLC7A5, SLC7A8, SLC7A11 and SLC1A5)[30](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR30 "Zhou, L. et al. Tumor cell-released kynurenine biases MEP differentiation into megakaryocytes in individuals with cancer by activating AhR-RUNX1. Nat. Immunol. 24, 2042–2052 (2023)."),[31](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR31 "Fiore, A. et al. Kynurenine importation by SLC7A11 propagates anti-ferroptotic signaling. Mol. Cell 82, 920–932 (2022)."),[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR32 "Sinclair, L. V., Neyens, D., Ramsay, G., Taylor, P. M. & Cantrell, D. A. Single cell analysis of kynurenine and system L amino acid transport in T cells. Nat. Commun. 9, 1981 (2018).") using small interfering RNA (siRNA)-mediated knockdown with KYN-FITC uptake assays in DLD1 cells. Knockdown of either SLC7A5 or SLC7A11 led to a modest reduction in KYN uptake, suggesting cooperative involvement of multiple transporters rather than one dominant pathway (Extended Data Fig. [3o,p](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig10)). Accordingly, PAT from MC38 iWAT tumours expressed higher levels of IDO1 and TDO2 than the tumour tissue itself (Fig. [3l](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig3)). Conditioned medium from PAT of mice treated with IDO1/TDO2 inhibitors (referred as adipose-CM + IDO/TDOi) failed to upregulate FTH1/FTL, an effect rescued by exogenous KYN (Fig. [3m](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig3)).

To determine whether KYN secretion is unique to PAT or adipose tissue in general, we compared PAT from MC38 tumour-bearing mice with contralateral iWAT (normal white adipose tissue (WAT)), analysing both tissue samples and their corresponding conditioned media. KYN levels were significantly higher in both PAT tissue and its conditioned medium (Fig. [3n,o](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig3)), confirming enhanced production specifically in the tumour-adjacent niche. To identify the cellular sources, we performed single-nucleus RNA sequencing (snRNA-seq) on PAT from MC38 iWAT tumours. This revealed that Ido1 was highly expressed in adipocytes, while Tdo2 was high in pre-adipocytes (pACs) and adipose-derived stem cells (ASCs) (Fig. [3p,q](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig3) and Supplementary Table [4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#MOESM5)). Because adipocytes, pACs and ASCs collectively constitute 54.8% of PAT cellularity (36%, 14.8% and 4%, respectively), we concluded they are the primary contributors to KYN production. These findings demonstrate that PAT establishes a KYN-rich TME to drive ferritin upregulation, iron sequestration and ferroptosis resistance. Collectively, these findings demonstrate that PAT confers ferroptosis resistance to tumour cells by establishing a KYN-rich TME, which promotes iron sequestration and suppresses ferroptosis through ferritin upregulation.

### KYN-derived 3-HK is responsible for PAT-mediated tumour ferroptosis resistance

KYN is taken up by cancer cells and metabolized into downstream products[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR33 "Wang, W. & Zou, W. Amino acids and their transporters in T cell immunity and cancer therapy. Mol. Cell 80, 384–395 (2020).") (Extended Data Fig. [4a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig11)). To determine whether KYN or its metabolites directly regulates ferritin, we systematically tested intermediates of the TRP–KYN pathway. Among them, KYN, 3-HK and 3-hydroxyanthranilic acid (3-HAA) were found to confer ferroptosis resistance to cancer cells (Extended Data Fig. [4b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig11)), and notably, these are sequential products of two enzymatic steps. To further reveal their roles, we genetically inhibited these metabolic steps. In KYN-supplemented cultures, knockdown of KYN monooxygenase (KMO), which converts KYN to 3-HK, reduced FTH1/FTL, increased Fe 2+ level, enhanced lipid peroxidation and sensitized cells to ferroptosis (Fig. [4a–d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig4) and Extended Data Fig. [4c–f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig11)). Conversely, silencing kynureninase (KYNU), which converts 3-HK to 3-HAA, potentiated KYN’s effects, further elevating FTH1/FTL, reducing Fe 2+ and lipid peroxidation, and protecting against cell death (Fig. [4e–h](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig4) and Extended Data Fig. [4g–j](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig11)). These opposing results strongly indicate that 3-HK is the key metabolite driving ferritin accumulation.

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x/figures/4)

Consistently, direct supplementation with 3-HK induced a dose-dependent upregulation of FTH1/FTL, decreasing labile iron, suppressing lipid peroxidation and enhancing ferroptosis resistance (Fig. [4i–l](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig4) and Extended Data Fig. [4k–n](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig11)). Next, we wondered by which mechanism 3-HK regulates ferritin. As neither 3-HK nor KYN affected FTH1/FTL mRNA (Extended Data Fig. [5a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig12)), we investigated whether this is regulated by post-translational regulation. Pulse-chase assays revealed that 3-HK delayed FTH1 degradation, indicating it stabilizes ferritin by inhibiting its breakdown (Extended Data Fig. [5b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig12)). As ferritin is degraded by NCOA4-mediated ferritinophagy[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR34 "Santana-Codina, N. et al. NCOA4-mediated ferritinophagy is a pancreatic cancer dependency via maintenance of iron bioavailability for iron–sulfur cluster proteins. Cancer Discov. 12, 2180–2197 (2022)."), we hypothesized that 3-HK stabilizes ferritin by inhibiting this process. Indeed, 3-HK treatment decreased lysosomal Fe 2+ in cancer cells (Fig. [4m](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig4)). Moreover, in NCOA4-knockout (KO) cells, both 3-HK and KYN failed to upregulate ferritin and lower intracellular Fe 2+ (Fig. [4n,o](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig4) and Extended Data Fig. [5c,d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig12)). The protective effect of 3-HK against RSL-3 was also attenuated in NCOA4-KO cells compared with controls (Fig. [4p,q](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig4)), confirming that NCOA4 is essential for the function of 3-HK. Of note, pretreatment with low-dose 3-HK (2.5 μM) conferred significant ferroptosis protection in MC38 and DLD1 control cells. By contrast, in NCOA4-KO MC38 cells and FTH1‑KO DLD1 cells, low concentrations of 3‑HK failed to confer protection, and partial rescue was observed only at higher concentrations (≥20 μM) (Extended Data Fig. [5e,f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig12)). This partial rescue at high concentrations might be attributed to the radical-trapping antioxidant (RTA) activity of 3-HK. Notably, even at high concentrations, the protective effect of 3-HK against ferroptosis remained stronger in control cells than in NCOA4-KO or FTH1-KO cells (Extended Data Fig. [5e,f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig12)). Consistently, both KYN and 3-HK exhibited only mild RTA activity at low concentrations (Extended Data Fig. [5h](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig12)). These findings indicate that, at low concentrations, the anti‑ferroptotic effect of 3‑HK critically depends on ferritin‑mediated iron metabolism modulation, whereas at higher concentrations, the anti-ferroptotic effect of 3-HK results from a combination of ferritinophagy suppression and RTA activity.

We further examined the involvement of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AHR), a primary target of TRP metabolites, and similarly found that AHR KO did not impair 3-HK-mediated FTH1/FTL upregulation (Extended Data Fig. [5i](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig12)), thereby excluding AHR involvement. Previous studies have reported that metabolites of KYN pathway can protect against ferroptosis by activating the NRF2/SLC7A11 antioxidant axis or through direct radical scavenging at high concentrations (50–200 μM)[31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR31 "Fiore, A. et al. Kynurenine importation by SLC7A11 propagates anti-ferroptotic signaling. Mol. Cell 82, 920–932 (2022)."),[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR35 "Liu, D. et al. Tryptophan metabolism acts as a new anti-ferroptotic pathway to mediate tumor growth. Adv. Sci. 10, e2204006 (2023)."). However, we found that low-dose 3-HK (5 μM) upregulates FTH1/FTL protein levels without altering SLC7A11 expression and maintained its ability to induce FTH1/FTL even in NRF2- and SLC7A11-KO cells (Extended Data Fig. [5j–l](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig12)), suggesting a mechanism independent of the canonical antioxidant pathway. Furthermore, 3-HK-mediated upregulation of FTH1/FTL was independent of CD44-mediated iron uptake (Extended Data Fig. [5m,n](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig12)). Taken together, these findings delineate a metabolite-driven axis whereby PAT-derived KYN is converted to 3-HK within tumour cells, leading to suppression of NCOA4-mediated ferritin degradation, reduced iron bioavailability and enhanced ferroptosis resistance.

### 3-HK directly binds NCOA4 and blocks ferritinophagy initiation

To further elucidate how 3-HK modulates ferritinophagy, we investigated its impact on the interaction between NCOA4 and FTH1, an essential step initiating ferritinophagy[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR12 "Galy, B., Conrad, M. & Muckenthaler, M. Mechanisms controlling cellular and systemic iron homeostasis. Nat. Rev. Mol. Cell Biol. 25, 133–155 (2024)."). Immunofluorescence (IF) analysis revealed that treatment with 3-HK or KYN markedly reduced the colocalization of FTH1 and NCOA4 in tumour cells (Fig. [5a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig5) and Extended Data Fig. [6a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig13)). Consistently, immunoprecipitation (IP) assays demonstrated that 3-HK weakened the binding between FTH1 and NCOA4 (Fig. [5b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig5) and Extended Data Fig. [6b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig13)). Notably, KYNU knockdown similarly reduced the binding between FTH1 and NCOA4, whereas knockdown of KMO exerted the opposite effect (Fig. [5c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig5)). Furthermore, we purified full-length NCOA4 (NCOA4-fl) and ferritin-binding domain (termed NCOA4 383–522) to conduct in vitro pull-down experiments[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR36 "Hoelzgen, F. et al. Structural basis for the intracellular regulation of ferritin degradation. Nat. Commun. 15, 3802 (2024)."). The results showed that 3-HK specifically inhibited the direct binding between FTH1 and NCOA4, whereas neither KYN nor the downstream metabolite 3-HAA exhibited this effect (Fig. [5d,e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig5)). In addition, thermal shift assay indicated that 3-HK stabilized NCOA4 in a dose-dependent manner against thermal denaturation (Fig. [5f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig5)), suggesting a direct physical interaction between 3-HK and NCOA4. As expected, IF analysis revealed a colocalization of biotinylated 3-HK and NCOA4 in tumour cells (Extended Data Fig. [6c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig13)). Moreover, our streptavidin pull-down and Duolink proximity ligation assay (PLA) revealed a interaction between 3-HK and NCOA4 (Fig. [5g–i](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig5)). We further characterized the direct interaction between 3-HK and NCOA4 using surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and biolayer interferometry (BLI) with both NCOA4-fl and NCOA4 383–522. The results demonstrated that 3-HK binds directly to both NCOA4 383–522 and NCOA4-fl, with dissociation constants of approximately 0.8 μM and 0.5 μM, respectively (Fig. [5j](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig5) and Extended Data Fig. [6d–f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig13)). By contrast, neither KYN nor 3-HAA showed detectable binding to NCOA4 (Fig. [5k,l](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig5)). Together, these findings demonstrate that 3-HK directly binds to NCOA4, disrupts its interaction with FTH1 and consequently inhibits ferritinophagy.

### Targeting KYN biosynthesis sensitizes PAT-associated tumours to ferroptosis and immunotherapy

Based on our observation upregulated IDO1/TDO2 in PAT, we hypothesized their pharmacological inhibition would reduce local KYN and, consequently, intratumoural 3-HK and ferritin. Accordingly, a treatment with IDO/TDO inhibitors (epacadostat and LM10) significantly lowered KYN in PAT and 3-HK in tumours (Extended Data Fig. [7a,b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig14)). Immunoblotting confirmed corresponding downregulation of FTH1, FTL and NCOA4, consistent with enhanced ferritinophagy (Extended Data Fig. [7c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig14)). Thus, inhibiting the KYN pathway in vivo effectively disrupts the ferritin accumulation axis in tumours.

We next evaluated the therapeutic potential of targeting IDO1/TDO2 to overcome ferroptosis resistance. In BALB/c mice with iWAT-implanted CT26 tumours, IDO1/TDO2 inhibitors enhanced GPX4 knockdown-induced ferroptosis (Fig. [6a,b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig6) and Extended Data Fig. [7d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig14)). Similar sensitization occurred in DLD1 xenografts in nude mice (Fig. [6c,d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig6) and Extended Data Fig. [7e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig14)). Extending to a CRC patient-derived xenograft (PDX) model adjacent to iWAT, IDO1/TDO2 inhibition also sensitized tumours to the ferroptosis inducer IKE (Fig. [6e,f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig6) and Extended Data Fig. [7f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig14)), and this synergistic effect was further confirmed in immunocompetent C57BL/6J mice bearing MC38 tumours (Fig. [6g,h](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig6) and Extended Data Fig. [7g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig14)).

Because anti-PD-1 therapy promotes CD8+ T cell-mediated ferroptosis[37](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#ref-CR37 "Wang, W. et al. CD8+ T cells regulate tumour ferroptosis during cancer immunotherapy. Nature 569, 270–274 (2019)."), we hypothesized that IDO1/TDO2 inhibition could sensitize PAT-associated tumours to anti-PD-1 treatment. Indeed, while anti-PD-1 monotherapy showed limited efficacy against iWAT-implanted tumours, the combination of IDO1/TDO2 inhibitors with anti-PD-1 antibody further enhanced tumour suppression. Notably, this enhanced suppression was reversed by Lip-1, confirming ferroptosis involvement (Fig. [6i,j](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig6) and Extended Data Fig. [7h](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig14)). The benefit of the combination was also validated in peritoneal metastasis models (Fig. [6k–m](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig6) and Extended Data Fig. [7i,j](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig14)). Thus, targeting IDO1/TDO2 represents a promising strategy to overcome ferroptosis resistance and enhance immunotherapy efficacy in PAT-rich cancers.

### PAT-associated ferritin regulatory axis correlates with malignancy and ICB response

To clinically validate the PAT–ferritin axis, we analysed radiographic cross-sectional imaging data from patients with GC who had peritoneal metastases. Compared with primary tumours, peritoneal lesions showed a poorer response to neoadjuvant chemo‑immunotherapy (Fig. [7a,b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig7)). IHC analysis of paired tissue specimens in another cohort revealed significantly higher expression of FTH1 and FTL, along with reduced levels of the lipid peroxidation marker 4-HNE, in peritoneal metastases compared with primary tumour sites (Fig. [7c,d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig7)). Furthermore, KMO high/KYNU low signature, reflecting elevated 3‑HK, correlated with advanced stage and worse survival in CRC and GC (Fig. [7e,f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig7)). These results indicate that activation of this axis (Fig. [7g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-026-01907-x#Fig7)) contributes to therapy resistance in PAT‑rich cancers and may serve as a predictive biomarker for immunotherapy outcome.
